pullRequest.title: Fix: saving compressed load files with .gz extension
pullRequest.body:
<!--
Thank you for submitting a pull request! Please provide a brief description of your changes below.
-->
### Description
This PR adds the `.gz` extension to compressed files based on a new config `disable_extension` that is set to `True` by default.

In a nutshell:
- `is_compression_disabled()` is removed and destination load jobs have access to `BufferedDataWriterConfiguration`. 
- Since the task involves getting rid of tricks like `FileStorage.is_gzipped()`, solely relying on  `BufferedDataWriterConfiguration` is not sufficient when it comes to imported files, since they must not be compressed in any situation. For this reason, a new context class `FileImportContext` was introduced that tracks whether a file is an imported file and, thus, does not require compression. The `is_compressed_file` attribute of `FileImportContext` is then accessed by the duckdb and clickhouse load jobs to correctly set the compression type.

### Related Issues

- Resolves #925

pullRequest.title: docs: add realistic closure-based data masking example
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Replaces the hardcoded column name example in the pseudonymizing columns documentation with a more realistic closure-based approach that works with all backends supported by the `sql_database` source.

## Changes

- Updated `/docs/website/docs/general-usage/customising-pipelines/pseudonymizing_columns.md`
- Replaced the dummy source example with a real-world `mask_columns()` function that uses closures
- Added support for all sql_database backends (PyArrow, ConnectorX, Pandas, SQLAlchemy)
- Added two masking methods: `MASK` (replace with a mask string) and `NULLIFY` (replace with NULL)
- Included usage examples with `sql_table` source

## Details

The new example demonstrates:
- Using closures to capture parameters (columns, method, mask) rather than hardcoding values
- A more realistic function that can be reused across different resources
- Proper handling of different data formats based on the backend
- Both MASK and NULLIFY methods for different use cases

This addresses issue #2116 by providing a practical example that users can adapt for their own data masking needs.

Closes #2116

pullRequest.title: feat: parallelize all sources in Airflow, including the first one
pullRequest.body:
Resolves #2196. Replaces the first source task with an EmptyOperator start node so all decomposed source components fan out concurrently in both parallel and parallel-isolated modes.

<!--
Thank you for submitting a pull request! Please provide a brief description of your changes below.
-->
### Description

Parallelizes all sources in Airflow, including the first one, for both `decompose="parallel"` and `decompose="parallel-isolated"` modes.

**Problem:** The first decomposed source component was used as the initial upstream task, forcing it to complete before the remaining sources could fan out. This added unnecessary wall-clock time equal to the duration of that first source.

**Solution:** Replaced the first source task with an `EmptyOperator` start node so all decomposed source components fan out concurrently from a shared no-op root.

**Before:** `first_source_task -> [remaining_sources in parallel] -> end`

**After:** `start_empty -> [all_sources in parallel] -> end_empty`

**Runtime improvement:** Overall DAG time shifts from `time(first) + max(time(others))` to `max(time(all))`.

**What does not change:**
- `decompose="none"` and `decompose="serialize"` behavior is unchanged.
- Shared-state caveat in `parallel` mode still applies; `parallel-isolated` remains safer for state isolation.
- Incremental loading warnings still fire in `parallel` mode.

**Tests:** All 12 tests in `test_airflow_wrapper.py` pass (including parallel, parallel-isolated, single-resource, incremental, serialize, retry, state wipe, callable, and multiple sources).

<!--
Please link any related issues. This helps us keep the PR focused and merge it faster.
-->
### Related Issues

- Resolves #2196

<!--
Provide any additional context about the PR here.
-->
### Additional Context

Files changed:
- `dlt/helpers/airflow_helper.py` -- core fan-out topology change in `parallel` and `parallel-isolated` branches of `PipelineTasksGroup.add_run()`
- `tests/helpers/airflow_tests/test_airflow_wrapper.py` -- updated task counts, dependency assertions, and pipeline-attach loops to match new DAG shape

<!--

pullRequest.title: Upsert merge strategy for iceberg
pullRequest.body:
<!--
Thank you for submitting a pull request! Please provide a brief description of your changes below.
-->
### Description
This PR enables the "upsert" merge write disposition for Iceberg. The functionality is similar to how delta works. Underneath, Iceberg's upsert table is used.

<!--
Please link any related issues. This helps us keep the PR focused and merge it faster.
-->
### Related Issues

- Resolves #2549 

<!--
Provide any additional context about the PR here.
-->
### Additional Context (for failing tests)
1.  The test `test_merge_on_keys_in_schema_nested_hints` would likely fail with maximum recursion error, as Iceberg's upsert has a limitation mentioned [here](https://github.com/apache/iceberg-python/issues/1759). This is apparently resolved in pyiceberg 0.9.1, but I'm still getting a sigill error with 0.9.1 and maximum recursion error with 0.9.0, the reason for which I'm unsure about.
    -  **Possible solution**: The issue can be addressed with batching the tables with size 1000 each.


2.  The test `test_table_format_schema_evolution` is failing with `Data type struct<a: int64> is not supported in join non-key field json`  because iceberg's upsert uses arrow's hash join which doesn't handle nested types. The issue was addressed with this [PR](https://github.com/apache/iceberg-python/pull/1878) in pyiceberg 0.9.1, but with 0.9.1 I get a different error saying `Unexpected physical type FIXED_LEN_BYTE_ARRAY for decimal(6, 4), expected INT32`. The latter issue was also addressed in this [PR](https://github.com/apache/iceberg-python/pull/1983) in pyiceberg, but it's not yet released. Therefore, the issue should go away with pyiceberg's next release. (I tested with the newest code and it works 👀 )
    -  **Possible solution**: We wait for the next pyiceberg release and upgrade to the latest version.

3. The tests `test_pipeline_load_parquet` and `test_table_format_child_tables ` fail because iceberg's upsert strictly forbids duplicates in input data as well as target table - if the key columns are not unique inside the source it raises an error. Delta doesn't even seem to be working properly 👀 .
   - **Possible solution**: We keep it explicit in the docs and leave it to the user to follow the uniqueness requirement + adjust the failing tests.

pullRequest.title: docs: adds documentation for column subset selection in sql_database source
pullRequest.body:
### Description

This PR adds documentation on how to select a subset of columns from database tables when using the `sql_database` source. It shows how to use the `table_adapter_callback` parameter to filter columns at the table definition level, ensuring that schema inference works correctly with only the selected columns.

### Related Issues

- Fixes #2551

### Additional Context

This documentation addresses a schema inference issue ([#2551](https://github.com/dlt-hub/dlt/issues/2551)) where using a `query_adapter_callback ` to select a subset of columns caused an `UnboundColumnException` during normalization. The normalizer incorrectly expected all columns from the original table schema, including non-nullable columns that were excluded from the selection.

While a code-level change to the inference logic was considered, it would introduce significant complexity. This PR instead documents a simple and effective workaround using `table_adapter_callback`. By modifying the table metadata directly, users can align the schema with the selected columns, providing a clean solution without altering the core logic (See https://github.com/dlt-hub/dlt/issues/2551#issuecomment-3063393177)

pullRequest.title: pyarrow: respect resource hints before extract
pullRequest.body:
Here's a PR summary:

---

## Fix: Apply user column hints before PyArrow extraction (fixes #2788)

### Problem

When using `sql_table()` with `backend="pyarrow"`, user-provided column hints via `apply_hints()` were not being applied before data extraction. PyArrow would use the reflected database types, ignoring user overrides like changing `decimal` to `double`.

```python
table = sql_table(..., backend="pyarrow")
table.apply_hints(columns={"numeric_col": {"data_type": "double"}})  # Was ignored!
```

### Solution

Merge user-provided column hints with SQL reflection hints **before** passing them to the `TableLoader`. This ensures PyArrow uses the user-specified types when extracting data.
- warn if backend is pyarrow and user provides dynamic hints (callable) as those can't be used. in that case fallback to table reflection 

### Changes

**`dlt/sources/sql_database/helpers.py`:**
- Added logic to merge `resource.columns` hints with reflection hints before extraction, *both for defer_reflect=True/False*
- Added warning when callable (dynamic) hints are provided with pyarrow backend
-

**`tests/load/sources/sql_database/test_sql_database_source.py`:**
- Added `test_pyarrow_applies_hints_before_extract` test
- Parameterized for `defer_reflect`
- verify if pipeline-schema and pyarrow-schema are in sync (and loader file format is applied)

### follow up issues ??
-**primary_key** hint no merged. in case of deferred-reflect the current fix only merges `column` hints from resource and there is code that 

```
# ... case deferred-reflect
... 
        # set the primary_key in the incremental
        if incremental and incremental.primary_key is None:
            primary_key = hints["primary_key"]
            if primary_key is not None:
                incremental.primary_key = primary_key
... # << if user supplied primary key hint its not being used
```

- **Callable hints**: Dynamic column hints (callables) cannot be merged statically and will be applied later during extraction. A warning is logged.

- **Pydantic models**: When used as columns, Pydantic creates a validator that has compatibility issues with PyArrow's data format (separate from this fix). <<< ai commment, i need to investigate still

---

pullRequest.title: Fix: Max table nesting is ignored for the first run when import schema path is specified
pullRequest.body:
<!--
Thank you for submitting a pull request! Please provide a brief description of your changes below.
-->
### Description
This PR attempts to resolve the root cause of the issue described in #2989, where the `max_table_nesting` is ignored during the first run of the pipeline in a situation where the import schema path is defined. The root cause consists in the following:

1. The `max_nesting` information is stored as part of the `x-normalizer` processing hints in the schema.
2. All processing hints are discarded when an import schema is being created for the first time.
3. The pipeline loads the schema from the import folder without processing hints, including the `max_nesting` setting.

**Approach:** Just preserve the `max_nesting` setting when creating an import schema for the first time, while discarding other processing hints.

**Possibly better approaches:** 
1. Ignore import schema when it is created for the first time or hasn't changed compared to when first created.
2. Since `max_nesting` is a user defined setting, we can put it outside the `x-normalizer` hint group as a normal table attribute. This way it won't be removed when the import schema is first created. This approach would involve a proper schema migration.

<!--
Please link any related issues. This helps us keep the PR focused and merge it faster.
-->
### Related Issues

- Resolves #2989

pullRequest.title: Feature: Introduce support of http based resources for fs source
pullRequest.body:
### Description

This PR introduces native support for HTTP-based data sources, such as those provided by CDNs like CloudFront. By leveraging fsspec's HTTPFileSystem, the filesystem source can now read remote files directly from http:// and https:// URLs without a prior local download. This enhancement makes it possible to easily ingest data from a wide range of public datasets.

### Related Issues

* Resolves #3028 

### Additional Context

A key challenge addressed in this implementation is the lack of a standard Last-Modified timestamp in HTTPFileSystem, which is crucial for dlt's filesystem source. To handle this, I added a [PR](https://github.com/fsspec/filesystem_spec/pull/1909) introducing Last-Modified property for http based resources ensuring the conformance with the main implementation.

pullRequest.title: Fix/3047 prevent same naming for staging and final datasets
pullRequest.body:
### Description

Created `create_dataset_names` static method and added validation to prevent a setup where final and staging datasets have the same name. 

**Changes:**
- Created `WithStagingDataset.create_dataset_names()` as a `@staticmethod`
- Added validation logic to raise `ValueError` when dataset names are equal
- Added error message with clear explanation of the issue and solution
- Added tests covering both success and error scenarios to ensure error message gets raised.

**Why this change:**
As mentioned in [ticket-3047](https://github.com/dlt-hub/dlt/issues/3047), the user has the ability to setup the staging dataset which could end up with the same name as the final dataset. A validation was added to ensure that a 'ValueError' gets raised when the stage dataset and final dataset have the same name otherwise a tuple is returned containing the data set name and the staging data set name.

<!--
-->

[ticket-3047](https://github.com/dlt-hub/dlt/issues/3047)

Provide any additional context about the PR here.
-->
### Additional Context

This change prevents potential data loss scenarios where users might accidentally configure staging and final dataset names to be identical. The validation ensures that setup commands that should only truncate staging datasets don't accidentally truncate the final dataset.

pullRequest.title: Docs: Forcing root key propagation section improved
pullRequest.body:
This PR adds an additional step to the [`Forcing root key propagation`](https://dlthub.com/docs/general-usage/merge-loading#forcing-root-key-propagation) section in `merge-loading.md`. The initial version suggests that this would work:

```py
import dlt

@dlt.resource
def my_table():
    yield {
        "id": 1,
        "name": "anuun",
        "age": 10, 
        "siblings": [
            {
                "id": 2,
                "name": "s",
            },
            {
                "id": 3,
                "name": "b",
            }
        ]
      }


@dlt.source()
def my_source():
    return [my_table()]

pipeline = dlt.pipeline(
    "test_pipeline",
    destination="duckdb",
    dataset_name="test_dataset",
)

the_source = my_source()

load_info = pipeline.run(the_source, write_disposition="replace")

the_source.root_key = True

load_info = pipeline.run(the_source, write_disposition="replace")
print(load_info)

```
However, this fails with the warning:
```
2025-09-05 16:11:07,691|[WARNING]|20067|8291859136|dlt|job_client_impl.py|_check_table_update_hints:769|Column(s) ['"_dlt_root_id"'] with NOT NULL are being added to existing table my_table__siblings. If there's data in the table the operation will fail.
```
as well as the error:
```
<class 'dlt.destinations.exceptions.DatabaseTransientException'>
Parser Error: Adding columns with constraints not yet supported
```

The reason for this is self-explanatory and stems from the fact that dlt attempts to add the `_dlt_root_id` column with a `NOT NULL` constraint to an existing schema.

The solution would be to drop the sources/resources with the drop command, rerun the pipeline with "replace" and root key enabled. This is now mentioned in the docs. 

Resolves #3062

pullRequest.title: QoL: accept destination name as shorthand form of destination
pullRequest.body:
This PR extends the `Destination.from_reference` so that if it encounters an `UnknownDestinationModule` exception, it tries to resolve the destination type from configs based on the destination name and then use the resolved type to build the destination. An appropriate test is added. 

See ticket for more context, but the main reason for this is the following:

As of now, when moving from local to production, people still need to change the destination in the code itself: 
For example, locally they run with duckdb:
```py
pipeline = dlt.pipeline("pipeline", destination="duckdb")
```
Then, in production, they need to run with Bigquery and therefore need to adjust:
```py
pipeline = dlt.pipeline("pipeline", destination="bigquery")
```

This PR enables the user just to set a destination name and control type in configs. Say, the user has:
```py
pipeline = dlt.pipeline("pipeline", destination="warehouse") 
```
They don't need to change the actual pipeline code and can simply control the destination type from configs:
```toml
[destination.warehouse]
#destination_type="duckdb" 
destination_type="bigquery"
```


Resolves #3109

pullRequest.title: fix/3141 - process Oracle "table not found" exception
pullRequest.body:
- Resolves #3141 

As seen from the issue and reproduced with `oracledb` driver, `SqlalchemyClient._make_database_exception` doesn't recognise `ORA-00942 table does not exist` to be rethrown as `DatabaseUndefinedRelation`, which is the expected behaviour for first runs when dlt tables don't exist on destination dataset. The reason is that `oracledb` internally doesn't raise neither `sa.exc.ProgrammingError` nor `sa.exc.OperationalError`, but instead raises the superclass generic `sa.exc.DatabaseError`. In this PR, I've added the logic to process only this case individually and raise `DatabaseUndefinedRelation`, otherwise de-facto resort to default logic in the next elif branch - raise generic `DatabaseTransientException`

Tested manually on local Oracle instance

pullRequest.title: Fix/3159 pydantic model incorrect serialization
pullRequest.body:
### Description

This PR adds an explicit `return_validated_models` option to the Pydantic `DltConfig` to control whether validated Pydantic model instances are returned during extraction instead of being converted to dictionaries.

**Problem:**
When using a Pydantic model as a validator with `@dlt.resource(columns=Model)`:
1. dlt validates the data using the Pydantic model
2. Always converts validated objects to dictionaries before passing them downstream
3. Transformers receive dicts instead of model instances
4. Users must manually reconstruct Pydantic objects in transformers

**Solution:**
This PR adds a new `DltConfig` option: `return_validated_models`. When set to `True`, the Pydantic validator returns validated model instances instead of converting them to dictionaries. This preserves backward compatibility (defaulting to `False`) while giving users explicit control over the output format.

**Usage:**
```python
from typing import ClassVar
from pydantic import BaseModel
from dlt.common.libs.pydantic import DltConfig
import dlt

class User(BaseModel):
    id: int
    name: str
    dlt_config: ClassVar[DltConfig] = {"return_validated_models": True}

@dlt.resource(columns=User)
def users():
    yield {"id": 1, "name": "Alice"}
    yield User(id=2, name="Bob")

@dlt.transformer(data_from=users)
def process_users(user: User):
    # user is a Pydantic model instance, not a dict
    print(f"Processing user: {user.name}")
    yield user
```

**Changes:**
- Added `return_validated_models` option to `DltConfig` in `dlt/common/libs/pydantic.py`
- Updated `PydanticValidator` in `dlt/extract/validation.py` to check the flag and return model instances when enabled
- Added unit tests for the extract validator and pipeline integration tests
- Updated documentation in `docs/website/docs/general-usage/resource.md`

### Related Issues

- Fixes #3159

---------------------

pullRequest.body:
This PR adjusts the `JsonLItemsNormalizer`'s `_coerce_null_value` so that it accommodates columns that have previously been flattened to compound columns in the same table.

pullRequest.title: feat: `snowflake` clustering key modifications
pullRequest.body:
### Description
Currently, the `snowflake` destination only interprets `cluster` hints on table creation. Any subsequent changes in `cluster` hints are ignored. This PR changes that, such that `cluster` hints are also interpreted on table alterations.

These three cases are supported:
(1) add clustering: without clustering -> with clustering
(2) change clustering: change set of cluster columns or column order
(3) drop clustering: with clustering -> without clustering

Note that there is no active hint change detection. Any changes in `cluster` hints are only processed when the current table alteration mechanism is triggered, which is only when a new column is added.

### Related Issues

- Closes #3306

pullRequest.title: 3312: feat(snowflake) - add column_comment/description hint support
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Add support for column comments in the Snowflake adapter, allowing users to set column descriptions that propagate to Snowflake.

- Add `escape_snowflake_literal()` function for proper SQL string escaping
- Add `COLUMN_COMMENT_HINT` constant (`x-snowflake-column-comment`) for Snowflake-specific hints
- Override `_get_column_def_sql()` to append `COMMENT` clause to column definitions
- Support both generic `description` field and Snowflake-specific hint for portability

This follows the exact pattern established by the Databricks adapter.

## Usage

```python
# Using generic description field (portable across destinations)
resource.apply_hints(columns={"my_column": {"description": "Column description"}})

# Using Snowflake-specific hint
from dlt.destinations.impl.snowflake.snowflake import COLUMN_COMMENT_HINT
resource.apply_hints(columns={"my_column": {COLUMN_COMMENT_HINT: "Snowflake-specific comment"}})
```

Generated SQL:
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_table (
    my_column VARCHAR COMMENT 'Column description'
)
```

## Test plan

- [x] Add `test_create_table_with_column_comments()` - tests both `description` and `COLUMN_COMMENT_HINT` fields
- [x] Add `test_column_comment_escaping()` - tests special character escaping (`'` → `''`)
- [x] Add `test_alter_table_with_column_comments()` - tests COMMENT in ALTER TABLE statements
- [x] `make format` passes
- [x] `make lint` passes (no new errors)

Fixes #3312

pullRequest.title: feat/3324-adding-iceberg-catalogs-compatibilities
pullRequest.body:
### Description

This PR removes the hardcoded in-memory SQLite catalog limitation and enables DLT to work with all PyIceberg-supported catalog types including REST catalogs, through leveraging the `load_catalog` functionality. The actual execution and operation of Iceberg format remains the same, we just load the catalog and pass them along to the pipeline.  

Previously, the Iceberg destination hardcoded catalog creation to use `sqlite:///:memory:`, limiting Iceberg support:

```python
catalog = get_sql_catalog(
    catalog_name or "default", 
    "sqlite:///:memory:",  # No other options!
    self.config.credentials
)
```

Leverage PyIceberg's built-in `load_catalog()` function to support all catalog types through standard configuration methods. The implementation provides a priority chain that tries multiple configuration sources and falls back gracefully.

In essence, this allows the user to add a `.pyiceberg.yaml` file to reference to their catalogs (which is in line with pyiceberg), similar to this: 
```yaml
# ~/.pyiceberg.yaml
catalog:
  production:
    type: rest
    uri: https://localhost:8181/catalog
    warehouse: analytics
```

The changes I made do not require the user to do much more than that, and they can continue using Iceberg as they were. 


### Related Issues

- Fixes #3324 

### Additional Context

To test it I run this for some time, monkey patching the core package. I then also added my contributions, built the package and tried it again through two of my prod pipelines. 

As for the tests, I run them locally, but I came across some what I think are broken tests -- because they had absolutely nothing to do with my changes, but I am gonna be checking them more in detail tomorrow.

pullRequest.title: feat: Support OAuth and base GCP credentials for BigQuery destination
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3380

- Add Union type to allow GcpOAuthCredentials and GcpCredentials
- Maintains backward compatibility with GcpServiceAccountCredentials
- Enables OAuth authentication for Workload Identity Federation
- Add tests for OAuth credentials acceptance

This change allows BigQuery destination to work with OAuth tokens from GitHub Actions Workload Identity Federation and other OAuth flows, without breaking existing service account authentication.

### Description

This PR enables `BigQueryClientConfiguration` to accept OAuth and base GCP credentials in addition to service account credentials. This change is motivated by the need to support modern authentication patterns like GitHub Actions Workload Identity Federation, where OAuth access tokens are provided via service account impersonation.

**Changes:**
- Modified `BigQueryClientConfiguration.credentials` type from `GcpServiceAccountCredentials` to `Union[GcpServiceAccountCredentials, GcpOAuthCredentials, GcpCredentials]`
- Added necessary imports for OAuth and base credential types
- Added two tests to verify OAuth credentials are accepted
- Fully backward compatible - all existing service account authentication continues to work

**Why this matters:**
- Aligns with Google's recommended Workload Identity Federation pattern (avoiding service account keys in secrets)
- Matches dbt-bigquery's multi-auth approach (`service-account`, `oauth`, `oauth-secrets`)
- Enables cloud-native CI/CD authentication without storing credentials

### Related Issues

- Fixes #3380

### Additional Context

This change has been successfully tested in production with:
- GitHub Actions + GCP Workload Identity Federation
- OAuth access tokens from impersonated service accounts
- Successful BigQuery loads using wrapped OAuth credentials
- Backward compatibility verified with existing service account credentials

The implementation is minimal (3 lines changed in configuration.py) and maintains full backward compatibility, so existing pipelines are unaffected.

pullRequest.title: overhaul support for Pydantic data validation
pullRequest.body:
## Breaking Changes
- Pydantic v1 support removed
- (ix) `data_type` contract semantic change: Now applies to type property changes on existing complete columns (not just variants). This could affect users who had data_type: freeze and were relying on being able to change nullable/precision/scale on existing columns — those will now be blocked. Note that that was done previously on `columns` which was wrong

## Description
- remove all Pydantic v1 compatibility code — the codebase now requires and uses only Pydantic v2 APIs
- add support for Pydantic models with discriminated union `RootModel` types in schema generation and contract enforcement
- rework schema contract handling to separate resource-defined hints from data-derived hints, so Pydantic model columns bypass contract checks when marked as authoritative
- rewrite `merge_table` to work without `diff_table`, fixing reference merging and treating `incremental` as a compound property
- suppress Pydantic deprecation warnings and fix instance-level attribute access to prepare for Pydantic v3
- resolves #3537 (if model authoritative)
- supports Pydantic models with discriminated fields to validate event streams
- allow Pydantic models on arrow and model items
- fully implement schema contracts on model items
- removes dead Pydantic 1 code paths
- resolves issues with Athena not falling back to append in some cases

pullRequest.title: fix(clickhouse): use CREATE OR REPLACE for merge temp tables
pullRequest.body:
### Description

On replicated ClickHouse clusters (including ClickHouse Cloud), `DROP TABLE ... SYNC` can take 2+ seconds due to replication coordination overhead. This causes significant latency in high-frequency pipelines using merge disposition.

This PR replaces the `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ... SYNC; CREATE TABLE ...` pattern with `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE`, which uses the Atomic database engine's atomic swap mechanism via `renameat2()`. The swap is nearly instant and the old table is cleaned up asynchronously.

### Related Issues

- Resolves #3562

### Additional Context

The Atomic database engine (default since ClickHouse 20.5, mandatory on Cloud) decouples table names from storage paths via UUIDs. `CREATE OR REPLACE` atomically swaps metadata entries — no need to wait for the old table deletion.

pullRequest.title: Fix/3637 cli info, show fail on custom destinations
pullRequest.body:
Fixes UnknownDestinationModule errors when running dlt pipeline <name> info/show on pipelines using `@dlt.destination`.          
                                                                                                                              
### Underlying problem                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                               
  `@dlt.destination` synthesizes a class in the caller's module (e.g. __main__.MyDestinationDestination). This path is
  non-importable from other processes (CLI, dashboard), causing dlt.attach() to fail.

### Changes

  - factory.py: Override destination_type to fall back to dlt.destinations.destination when the class lives in __main__
  - configuration.py: Clearer error message explaining the cross-process limitation
  - dashboard/utils/pipeline.py: Catch ConfigurationValueError so dashboard doesn't crash
  - destination.md: Added "CLI and dashboard limitations" section
  - Tests: Cover __main__ fallback, CLI info command, and dashboard operations for all three custom destination patterns
  (decorator, callable, string ref)

Resolves #3637
